title: ASPIRE-Unlearn: Privacy-Targeted Basis Selection Under Independent Auditing for Approximate Unlearning
description: ASPIRE-Unlearn is a narrow extension of GU/OrthoGrad-style retain-projection unlearning, not a new unlearning family. It builds the protected low-rank retain basis from a disjoint retained slice predicted to be privacy-vulnerable, then evaluates the claim under an audit protocol that uses one shard for checkpointing and a separate untouched shard for final privacy reporting.
motivation: Approximate unlearning can improve forgetting while making some retained users easier to identify, and existing retain-projection methods mostly protect retain geometry globally rather than the retained examples most likely to become privacy-exposed. The gap is a feasible, independently audited study of whether privacy-targeted basis selection actually improves retained-user privacy beyond untargeted projection under a single-GPU budget.
proposed_approach: Wrap a short-horizon gradient-unlearning baseline with two tightly scoped changes. First, on a disjoint retained selector split, score each retained example by forget-overlap sensitivity s(x)=g_r(x)^T C_f g_r(x), keep the top-scoring slice, and build a weighted low-rank protected basis only from that slice before projecting unlearning updates away from it; second, enforce independent auditing by selecting checkpoints only on R_audit_A within a retrain-calibrated forgetting band and reporting final retained-membership privacy only on untouched R_audit_B. The core ablation compares this targeted score against a matched-size untargeted subset to test whether the gain comes from the vulnerability score itself rather than any reduced-rank basis.

related_work.title: Geometric-disentangelment Unlearning
related_work.difference: This is the closest global retain-basis baseline. ASPIRE keeps projection as the mechanism but changes the source of the protected basis from global retain geometry to a retained slice chosen for predicted privacy vulnerability.

related_work.title: Go Beyond Your Means: Unlearning with Per-Sample Gradient Orthogonalization
related_work.difference: OrthoGrad orthogonalizes each update against retain gradients online. ASPIRE instead precomputes a static low-rank basis from a disjoint selector split and asks whether targeted basis construction is better aligned with retained privacy.

related_work.title: Unified Gradient-Based Machine Unlearning with Remain Geometry Enhancement
related_work.difference: Remain-geometry methods preserve retain utility globally. ASPIRE makes the narrower claim that the protected retain subspace should be chosen for retained privacy risk rather than average retain stability.

related_work.title: Rectifying Privacy and Efficacy Measurements in Machine Unlearning: A New Inference Attack Perspective
related_work.difference: This paper motivates stronger privacy evaluation. ASPIRE borrows that lesson by separating selector, stopping, and final reporting shards, but keeps the contribution on the defense side.

related_work.title: WARP: Weight Teleportation for Attack-Resilient Unlearning Protocols
related_work.difference: WARP is a broader privacy-aware unlearning protocol and defense paper. ASPIRE is a smaller empirical claim centered specifically on privacy-targeted basis selection under independent auditing.

related_work.title: Membership Inference Attacks From First Principles
related_work.difference: Provides the standard Gaussian LiRA methodology used for low-FPR retained-membership evaluation; ASPIRE does not propose a new attack.

related_work.title: When Machine Unlearning Jeopardizes Privacy
related_work.difference: Establishes the retained-privacy failure mode that motivates the problem, but does not study privacy-targeted retain-basis selection.

hypothesis: Retained privacy degradation after approximate unlearning is concentrated in a small subset of retained examples whose gradients align strongly with forget-gradient covariance. Building the protected low-rank basis from that vulnerable retained slice, and auditing it with a strictly held-out final shard, will reduce retained-member LiRA leakage relative to the base method and at least one untargeted retain-projection baseline at comparable forgetting quality.
success_criteria: On Purchase100 and CIFAR-10 under random 1 percent deletion, ASPIRE lowers retained-member LiRA TPR@1%FPR on R_audit_B relative to the base unlearning method while staying inside the retrain-calibrated forgetting band.
ASPIRE beats at least one of GU or OrthoGrad on both random-deletion settings and is not more than 5 percent worse than the stronger of them on either setting.
On CIFAR-10 high-loss 5 percent deletion, ASPIRE shows its largest relative privacy gain on the held-out final audit shard.
A matched-size untargeted-subset ablation performs worse than full ASPIRE, supporting the claim that the vulnerability score itself matters beyond any reduced-rank retained subset.
ASPIRE stays within 1.35x runtime of the base method and does not rely on R_audit_B for stopping, threshold fitting, or model choice.
